food_subgroup: Cephalopod seafood dish, stir-fried with numbing-spicy Sichuan pepper sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cuttlefish food product class but no specific entry for tengjiao-spiced baby cuttlefish preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cuttlefish as a commodity. No specific code exists for the tengjiao spiced preparation; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for tengjiao baby cuttlefish as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tengjiao baby cuttlefish is approximately 250 g, consisting of whole small cuttlefish wok-tossed in a numbing-spicy tengjiao pepper and chilli sauce, served as a shared dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cuttlefish, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15147) adapted with sauce component estimation from USDA Foundation Foods for oil and seasoning additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked cuttlefish (NDB 15147: protein ~16.2 g, fat ~0.7 g per 100 g raw, adjusted for cooking). Sauce components (cooking oil ~5 g/100 g dish, soy sauce sodium contribution ~300 mg/100 g dish) estimated from USDA Foundation Foods entries for soybean oil and soy sauce. Final per-100 g values represent the composite dish. Energy calculated by Atwater factors. This is an adapted estimate; no direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was found for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — seafood and protein-dominant dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tengjiao baby cuttlefish. Cuttlefish and squid are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate seafoods. By analogy with other cooked seafood and protein-dominant dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI typically 0–10 for pure protein foods), a GI of 5 is assigned. The sauce contributes minimal carbohydrate (~3.2 g/100 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.4 per 250 g serving (GL = GI/100 × carb_g_per_serving = 5/100 × 8.0 = 0.4). gi_category is low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~520 mg/100 g; ~1300 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full restaurant serving can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~5), with high protein supporting satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tengjiao baby cuttlefish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its very low carbohydrate load and high protein content have minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose; however, sodium from the sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~520 mg/100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~112 mg/100 g) from cuttlefish, partially offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, cuttlefish provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial to heart health, but the high sodium content from the sauce warrants portion control and reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tengjiao Baby Cuttlefish (腾椒墨鱼仔) is a Chinese seafood dish featuring whole small cuttlefish wok-tossed in a fragrant, numbing-spicy sauce made with tengjiao (藤椒, green Sichuan pepper), dried chilli, garlic, ginger, and soy sauce. The dish originates from Sichuan-influenced Chinese cooking and is popular in Singapore's Chinese restaurant scene, including at establishments such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局). The cuttlefish are prized for their bouncy, Q-elastic texture (Q弹) and fresh oceanic flavour, which contrasts with the intense numbing (麻) and spicy (辣) aromatics of the tengjiao pepper. Tengjiao differs from standard red Sichuan peppercorn in that it delivers a fresher, more citrus-forward numbing sensation. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~13.5 g/100 g), low in carbohydrates (~3.2 g/100 g), and moderate in fat (~4.8 g/100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI ~5). It is a notable source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol. The primary nutritional concern is sodium (~520 mg/100 g) contributed by soy sauce and seasoning, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains mollusc and soy allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without verification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tengjiao cuttlefish?
Is 腾椒墨鱼仔 suitable for diabetics?
Is baby cuttlefish high in sodium?
What is the difference between tengjiao and mala?
Can I eat cuttlefish if I have high blood pressure?
What allergens are in tengjiao baby cuttlefish?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for tengjiao baby cuttlefish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked cuttlefish data with sauce component estimation. GI is an analogous estimate based on the protein-dominant, very low-carbohydrate profile of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cuttlefish) is well-characterised in USDA data and the sauce contribution is a defensible estimation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA SR Legacy cooked cuttlefish (NDB 15147) plus sauce component estimation; no direct Singapore-specific laboratory analysis was available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy cuttlefish entry and is not expected to be significant in this preparation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA data for cuttlefish and plant-based cooking oils used in this preparation. 4. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tengjiao baby cuttlefish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is unverified for this specific restaurant preparation; consumers requiring halal certification should confirm with the restaurant. 6. Cholesterol content (~112 mg/100 g) is notable; individuals with hypercholesterolaemia should moderate intake. 7. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局), Singapore, at SGD 24.70 per portion. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cuttlefish data and are not -1 as the source provides these values for the base ingredient.
